title: The strange way cocaine water pollution is changing salmon
summary:
It turns out that salmon exposed to cocaine through water pollution do a lot of swimming—which may not be a good thingStrange but true: salmon on cocaine swim a lot more.
This sounds like an odd thing to research, but cocaine and its metabolites—along with other drugs and pharmaceuticals—are widespread in the environment, mainly from human wastewater.
In the new study, Brand and his colleagues focused on wild Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar).
In one group of 35, the implant slowly released cocaine, in another it released benzoylecgonine (cocaine’s main metabolite), and in a control group it released nothing.
And recent research demonstrated how cocaine and its metabolites can accumulate in wild fish such as sharks.

title: 6 Facts About Climate Change
summary:
Read on to learn more about the history and consequences of climate change, as well as how it might be averted.
Research has shown that by the late 1970s, the oil company Exxon was aware that its fossil fuels could cause climate change that could have “dramatic environmental effects.” In 1988, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) was formed to address the Earth's rising temperatures.
Most people believe climate change is realThere is indeed a small, but noisy, group of people who believe climate change is not real.
However, in reality, the majority of people believe in climate change and would like to see actions taken to fix it.
“To avoid the worst consequences of climate change, we’ll need to reach ‘net zero’ carbon emissions by 2050 or sooner.

title: Why do gray whales keep dying in San Francisco's waters?
summary:
While a lack of food may be driving whales into the bay, it's not necessarily starvation that's killing them.
In recent years, nearly one-fifth of the gray whales that have swum into the San Francisco Bay have died there, usually after being struck by ships, according to Slaathaug's study published in the journal Frontiers in Marine Science this week.

title: Common cleaning sponge found to release trillions of microplastic fibers
summary:
As these sponges wear down, they can release tiny plastic particles into the environment.
Researchers estimate that more than a trillion microplastic fibers may be released worldwide every month as people use and discard these everyday cleaning tools.
These fragments can break down further into microplastic fibers, which are tiny strands of plastic often too small to see.
Denser sponges held up better over time and released fewer microplastic fibers, while less dense versions broke down more quickly.
Using Amazon sales from August 2023 as a reference point, they calculated that approximately 1.55 trillion microplastic fibers could be released each month.

title: Leicester team finds ancient freshened water under ocean floor
summary: The water is thought to be tens of thousands of years old, and while the process of how it got there is not yet fully understood, it is hoped analysis of the samples will help scientists learn more about climate change.

title: A mine despoiled the beauty of the rainforest. This Goldman Prize winner took action
summary:
This Goldman Prize winner took actiontoggle caption Goldman Environmental PrizeTheonila Roka Matbob was born in what should have been a lush rainforest.
"You have to go miles — into another region and territory — to find the trees, the forest," says Roka Matbob, who is now 35.
Her work as an activist to repair the environmental and social harms has earned her The Goldman Environmental Prize for 2026.
"Theonila is leading a historic effort to obtain justice for decades of environmental and social devastation because of the Panguna mine," says Ilan Kayatsky, of the Goldman Environmental Prize, in a statement to NPR.
toggle caption Goldman Environmental PrizeThis Goldman honor comes with a cash prize.

title: Sharks and tuna are overheating and running out of options
summary:
As ocean temperatures climb, these species are more likely to overheat, which could shrink their habitable range and push them toward cooler regions closer to the poles.
What Makes "Mesothermic" Fish UniqueThe study, published in the journal Science, focuses on "mesothermic" fishes.
These are a rare group, making up less than 0.1% of all fish species, that can retain body heat and keep parts of themselves warmer than the surrounding water.
These thresholds represent the water temperatures at which large fish can no longer release heat quickly enough to maintain stable body temperatures.
Looking ahead, the researchers expect that suitable habitats for large mesothermic fish will shrink as global temperatures continue to rise, especially during warmer months.
